preFix.title: Reverse-felting cut-out design
preFix.subtitle: Cut geometric or leaf shapes into a two-colour wet-felted slab to reveal the contrasting colour beneath
preFix.excerpt: Lay two contrasting colours of roving in separate layers, wet-felt them as a single slab, then cut away the top layer in shapes to expose the second colour below. The cut edges re-felt during finishing to produce a smooth, integrated surface with no raw edges. Works well for simple geometric patterns and bold leaf shapes.
preFix.sourceNotes: Observation-led; reverse-felting (inlay cut-away) is used in Scandinavian and Central Asian felt-making traditions and is taught in intermediate wet-felting curricula.

content.text: Reverse felting works by building a two-layer slab of
roving

with two distinct colours, one on top of the other. After the initial felting pass bonds the layers together, you cut through the top layer only and remove the cut pieces, leaving the lower colour exposed in the shape of the cut-outs. A final
fulling

pass re-seals the cut edges so they become part of the fabric surface rather than raw cuts. The technique produces a two-colour flat panel with shapes appearing as windows of the lower colour within the upper-colour ground. A 25 x 25 cm panel is a manageable size for a first piece.
Choosing your colours and design

Choose two colours with strong tonal contrast: light on top over dark below, or a warm colour over a cool one. The cut-out shapes appear in the lower colour, so the lower colour is the design colour. Simple bold shapes work best: squares, diamonds, elongated leaf ovals, or equilateral triangles in a grid. Thin cut lines and small or intricate shapes tend to close up or merge during fulling, so keep all cut-out shapes at least 2.5 cm across in the pre-cut panel. The shapes will shrink approximately 20 percent during the post-cut fulling pass, so cut them slightly larger than the planned finished size.
What you need

attrs.name: Hot water, around 50 to 55 degrees C
Felting mat, at least 40 x 40 cm
Felting net, at least 45 x 45 cm
Sharp pointed scissors, for precise cuts
Paper template for the cut-out shapes (optional but recommended)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Lay the lower colour layer

Lay the felting mat on a stable surface. Pull thin wisps from the lower-colour roving and lay them horizontally across a 36 x 36 cm area in two layers, then add two layers at 90 degrees. This gives four layers of lower-colour roving in total. Keep the layers even and the edges neat. Do not fold the edge wisps back yet; you will add the top-colour layers first.
Step 2: Lay the top colour layer

Without disturbing the lower layer, lay the top-colour roving directly on top in the same manner: two horizontal layers, then two vertical layers, all in the contrasting colour. The top colour must cover the entire lower-colour surface. When both sets of layers are down, fold the overhanging edge wisps from both colours back on themselves to form a neat double-thickness border. Cover the whole stack with the felting net.
Step 3: Wet out and initial felt

Wet the panel slowly and evenly with hot soapy water. Press down with flat palms to saturate without displacing the layered colours. Rub through the net steadily for 5 to 6 minutes. The two layers need to bond to each other during this initial pass; this is the most important stage before cutting. Lift the net and test bonding by pinching the surface and trying to separate the two layers. They should resist separation. If they peel apart easily, replace the net and rub for a further 3 minutes. Once the layers are bonded but the panel is not yet fully felted (it should be firm but still flexible), stop and proceed to cutting.
Step 4: Mark and cut the design

While the panel is still damp (but firm enough to cut cleanly), mark the cut-out shapes on the surface of the top layer. A water-erasable fabric marker works well; alternatively, press a paper template lightly onto the surface and cut around it. Insert the scissors at one edge of the planned cut-out, angle the blades so they cut only through the top layer, and cut out the shape. The two layers should be bonded enough to hold together during cutting but still distinguishable: you will feel the slight resistance difference between the two layers as the scissors pass through. Lift away the cut piece to reveal the lower colour beneath. Work through all planned cut-outs in the same way.
Step 5: Final fulling

Re-cover the cut panel with the net and rub again with hot soapy water for 3 minutes to begin sealing the cut edges. The raw cut fibres on the top-layer boundary and the exposed lower-layer fibres within the cut-out will both be matting together during this pass. Then roll the panel in the bamboo mat for 4 minutes per pass, rotating 90 degrees between passes, until the panel reaches approximately 25 x 25 cm. The cut edges will seal and the contrast windows will have crisp boundaries. Shock with cold water twice during the fulling stage.
Step 6: Rinse and dry

Rinse in lukewarm water until no soap remains. Roll in a tea towel and press out excess water; do not wring. Lay flat on a dry towel, pull to square, and allow to dry at room temperature. Inspect the cut edges when dry: well-executed edges feel smooth to the touch and show no fraying. Any edge that has not fully sealed can be dampened with warm soapy water and rubbed with a fingertip in a small circle to re-felt it. The finished panel can be used as a cushion front, a framed textile, or trimmed into smaller pieces.
Common problems

attrs.fix: The layers must resist separation before cutting. If they have separated, re-wet with hot soapy water, press the layers back together firmly, cover with net, and rub for another 3 minutes before continuing the fulling stage.
attrs.cause: Cutting was done too early, before the layers had bonded sufficiently in the initial felting pass
attrs.problem: The two layers separated at the cut edges rather than fusing smoothly

attrs.fix: Cut when the panel is partially felted and still has 30 to 40 percent of its shrinkage remaining. If cutting too early means the layers aren't bonded, increase the initial rubbing time rather than cutting later.
attrs.cause: Cutting was done late in the felting process when the panel had already shrunk significantly
attrs.problem: The cut-out shapes are much smaller than planned in the finished panel

attrs.fix: Use four full layers for each colour. For the next piece, choose colours with stronger tonal contrast. If the bleed-through is minor, it adds a subtle layered depth and is not a defect.
attrs.cause: The top layer was too thin — one or two layers instead of four — or the two colours were very close in tone, making bleed-through visible
attrs.problem: The lower colour shows through slightly even in uncut areas of the top layer
